name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC designs, manufactures, and operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites (~200 spacecraft), capturing daily imagery of the entire planet at up to 3.5-meter resolution. The company delivers geospatial data and AI-powered analytics through a cloud-based platform to agriculture, defense, energy, forestry, finance, and government customers. Products include the SuperDove daily monitoring constellation, SkySat and Pelican high-resolution tasking satellites, and the Tanager hyperspectral imaging constellation. Founded in 2010 as a Public Benefit Corporation, headquartered in San Francisco, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet launched the Pelican next-generation high-resolution satellite constellation and the Tanager hyperspectral imaging satellites in 2024-2025, adding entirely new spectral and resolution capabilities to its fleet [1]. The company also executed a strategic pivot from a hardware/imagery company to an AI-powered data analytics platform, with defense and intelligence revenue growing more than 50% YoY in FY2026 [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): Planet declared '2026 is the year of AI for Planet,' positioning itself as a 'Space and AI' company with AI-powered change detection, object identification, and analytics integrated into its platform, though specific AI-attributable revenue figures have not been separately disclosed [2]. Market Share (2/2): Planet operates the world's largest commercial Earth-imaging fleet with ~200 satellites, and is the only company imaging the entire landmass of Earth every single day at 3.5m resolution — a category it created and continues to define with no direct competitor matching its daily revisit cadence [1][2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): FY2027 guidance of $415-440M (35%+ growth) and $900M in backlog (up 79% YoY) provide strong revenue visibility, with Pelican and Tanager-2 constellations in active development to extend resolution and spectral capabilities [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Planet's R&D spending as a percentage of revenue significantly exceeds aerospace and defense industry averages, reflecting the capital-intensive demands of next-generation satellite development and AI analytics capabilities [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's public benefit purpose — 'to accelerate humanity toward a more sustainable, secure, and prosperous world by illuminating environmental and social change' — is specific, deeply intentional, and legally codified through its PBC structure with a dual-class share arrangement that protects long-term mission focus from short-term shareholder pressure [3]. The purpose has guided 16 years of consistent execution building the world's largest Earth-imaging fleet, though the company has not yet faced the multi-decade, multi-leadership-transition validation that distinguishes a 4. Community Impact (1/2): Planet provides open data access to thousands of researchers and NGOs, supported the NICFI tropical forest monitoring program for deforestation tracking, and its core product enables environmental monitoring for climate action, disaster response, and humanitarian operations worldwide [3]. Employee Retention (1/2): Co-founder and CEO Will Marshall has led the company for 16 years since founding, providing exceptional leadership continuity, and the ~945-person team operates in a culture described as 'passionate about hard problems' with no evidence of mass layoffs or significant turnover events through the company's pre-profit years [2]. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling of approximately $5.1M over the most recent three months, with no meaningful net insider buying identified despite Marshall's direct holdings increasing from 11.69M to 12.92M shares (likely attributable to equity vesting rather than open-market purchases) [4].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available growth-stock metrics. Planet delivered record FY2026 revenue of $307.7M (up 26% YoY) with Q4 revenue surging 41% YoY to $86.8M, achieving its first fiscal year of Adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow profitability with $57.6M FCF and $640M in cash [1]. However, GAAP profitability remains deeply negative with a -80.2% net profit margin driven by heavy stock-based compensation and satellite asset depreciation. D/E of 245% reflects $462M in convertible debt against a small equity base, though offset by $640M cash. ROE is -78.4% on a GAAP basis. Revenue growth metrics are the clear strength: 41.1% Q4 YoY growth and a 3-year revenue CAGR of 17.2% both exceed targets. Forward P/E and PEG are excluded as the company has negative trailing and near-zero forward earnings (-$0.02 forward EPS). Scored on 5 available metrics (7 weight units): D/E 0.237(1x) + ROE 0.000(2x) + RevYoY 1.000(1x) + RevCAGR 1.000(2x) + PM 0.000(1x) = 3.237/7 = 0.462 × 10 = 5 [5].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Planet's core business is structurally immune to AI disruption — the company operates a physical constellation of ~200 satellites in low Earth orbit that capture proprietary imagery no software model can replicate or substitute [1][2]. AI cannot replace the need for actual sensors in orbit collecting real-world geospatial data; it can only consume more of it. AI is a powerful demand driver for Planet: as AI models become more capable, the appetite for high-cadence, high-quality Earth observation training data accelerates, making Planet's daily global imagery increasingly valuable as an irreplaceable input layer for defense, agriculture, climate, and insurance AI applications. The physical satellite infrastructure, orbital mechanics expertise, and 16 years of accumulated imagery archives create a moat that no purely digital competitor can breach [2]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet generates meaningful AI-related revenue through its analytics platform, which applies AI-powered change detection, object identification, and environmental monitoring to its proprietary satellite imagery, and the company explicitly positions itself as a 'Space and AI' company with AI capabilities embedded in its data delivery pipeline driving the FY2027 growth outlook of $415-440M [1][2].

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs is a textbook infinite game player. Co-founder Will Marshall has led the company for 16 years with an unwavering mission to image the entire Earth daily — a purpose so bold it can never be fully achieved. The company's PBC structure legally binds it to its public benefit purpose, and strategic decisions consistently prioritize long-term capability over quarterly results. Planet invested through 14 consecutive years of operating losses to build the world's largest Earth-imaging fleet, sacrificing near-term profitability to establish an irreplaceable data asset that is now reaching its profitability inflection point.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (3/3): Planet's purpose — 'to accelerate humanity toward a more sustainable, secure, and prosperous world by illuminating environmental and social change' — meets every criterion of Sinek's Just Cause definition: it is resilient (endures beyond any single satellite or product), inclusive (invites governments, NGOs, researchers, and businesses to contribute), service-oriented (benefits humanity's understanding of Earth systems), idealistic (a future of complete planetary awareness does not yet exist), and big (daily imaging of the entire Earth can never be 'completed') [3]. The PBC corporate structure legally binds the company to this purpose, and 16 years of consistent execution — building the world's largest Earth-imaging fleet from scratch — validates that the cause shapes every strategic decision.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Planet operates with a stable leadership team anchored by co-founder Will Marshall's 16-year tenure as CEO, and the company's culture emphasizes learning, teaching, dialogue, and 'owning mistakes' — language consistent with psychological safety [2]. The ~945-employee team has not experienced the mass layoffs common among growth-stage tech companies, and the 'Silicon Valley meets Aerospace' ethos suggests creative empowerment. However, no specific voluntary turnover rates, average tenure data, or employee survey results are publicly available to confirm exceptional trust levels beyond industry norms.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Planet consistently frames its competitive positioning around differentiation rather than destruction — emphasizing daily revisit frequency as its unique strength while acknowledging that competitors like Maxar and Airbus offer superior per-image resolution [2]. Management's competitive language focuses on 'what only Planet can do' (daily global coverage) rather than attacking rivals' weaknesses, and the company has pursued partnerships and data integration with other satellite providers rather than treating the market as zero-sum.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Planet has meaningfully evolved its business model from a hardware company selling satellite imagery to an AI-powered data analytics platform, adding Tanager hyperspectral satellites to expand beyond optical imaging and pivoting aggressively into defense and intelligence (which grew 50%+ in FY2026) [1]. The shift from imagery provider to analytics platform represents genuine strategic flexibility. However, these pivots are evolutionary extensions of the core satellite mission rather than the kind of transformative 'blow up the business model' flexibility Sinek describes — Planet has always been about imaging the Earth from space.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Will Marshall and the Planet leadership team invested through 14 consecutive years of operating losses to build the world's largest Earth-imaging fleet, consistently choosing long-term capability over short-term profitability in a capital-intensive industry where most competitors failed [1]. The company committed billions in cumulative spending to launch and replenish ~200 satellites before reaching its first year of free cash flow positivity in FY2026. Additionally, Planet's decision to incorporate as a Public Benefit Corporation — legally binding itself to stakeholder interests beyond shareholder returns — required genuine courage, as it introduced governance constraints that most growth-stage companies avoid [3].

investment_thesis: Planet Labs reached a critical inflection point in FY2026, delivering $307.7M in revenue (up 26% YoY) and achieving its first fiscal year of Adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow profitability with $57.6M FCF and $640M in cash reserves [1]. Q4 revenue surged 41% YoY to a record $86.8M, with defense and intelligence revenue growing more than 50% as government demand for persistent Earth observation accelerated. The $900M backlog (up 79% YoY) and FY2027 guidance of $415-440M (35%+ growth) signal sustained momentum as Planet transitions from a capital-intensive satellite deployment phase to a high-margin recurring data analytics business [1]. The company's unmatched asset — the only commercial fleet imaging the entire Earth daily — becomes more valuable as AI models consume ever-larger volumes of geospatial training data, positioning Planet as an irreplaceable infrastructure layer in the AI ecosystem [2].
thesis_continued: The primary concern is valuation relative to profitability. At $41.62 per share with a $14.8B market cap, Planet trades at approximately 48x FY2026 revenue and has no meaningful trailing earnings, reflecting a substantial premium on future growth and the recent 54% stock price surge from $27 levels [5]. GAAP profitability remains deeply negative at -80.2% net margin due to heavy stock-based compensation and satellite depreciation, and the path to GAAP breakeven remains multi-year. The D/E ratio of 245% reflects $462M in convertible debt, though this is mitigated by $640M in cash. Net insider selling of $5.1M in recent months raises questions about management's near-term conviction at current prices [4]. Commercial sector revenue was down modestly YoY in FY2026 while the NICFI Norway contract ended, indicating concentration risk in defense/intelligence that could create revenue lumpiness if government budget priorities shift [1].
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue guidance of $415-440M (35%+ growth) supported by $900M backlog, with defense and intelligence contracts expected to continue growing 50%+ as geopolitical demand for persistent Earth observation accelerates globally

AI platform monetization accelerating as Planet transitions from imagery provider to AI-powered analytics business, unlocking higher-margin recurring revenue streams and expanding total addressable market beyond traditional satellite imagery buyers

Pelican and Tanager-2 next-generation satellite constellations enhancing resolution and hyperspectral capabilities, enabling premium pricing and entry into new verticals including methane detection, mineral exploration, and precision agriculture
key_risks: Valuation at ~48x FY2026 revenue with no GAAP profitability prices in years of flawless execution — any deceleration in the defense/intelligence growth engine or failure to convert the $900M backlog could trigger severe multiple compression from current $14.8B market cap

GAAP net margin of -80.2% driven by heavy stock-based compensation dilutes existing shareholders annually, and the path to GAAP breakeven requires sustained revenue scaling while controlling SBC growth — a challenge few pre-profit growth companies manage successfully

Commercial sector revenue declined modestly in FY2026 and the NICFI Norway contract ended, concentrating growth in government/defense contracts that introduce budget cycle dependency, political risk, and potential revenue lumpiness from large contract timing
